text: Isolated caudate lobe resection, inferior vena cava tumor removal, right adrenalectomy, and aortocaval lymph node dissection. A patient was recurrent caudate lobe hepatocellular carcinoma, inferior vena cava tumor thrombosis and aortocaval lymph node metastases after bisegmentectomy of segment V and VI.
The surgeon begins liver mobilization and lyses adhesions, exploring the caudate lobe. Retro-hepatic inferior vena cava dissection is performed from the caudate lobe of the liver.
The surgeon applies the Pringle maneuver to control blood flow, followed by the transection of the liver parenchyma.

Intraoperative ultrasonography reveals a tumor thrombus in the inferior vena cava (IVC). The surgeon performs total hepatic vascular exclusion, applying the Pringle's maneuver followed by proximal and distal IVC clamping.

The surgeon performs a cavotomy and partial resection of the inferior vena cava (IVC) wall, addressing the chordate lobe tumor. The IVC is then repaired using a continuous running suture with Prolene.

The surgeon begins an aortocaval lymph node dissection starting from the retrocaval area. The right adrenal gland is removed using a bipolar sealing device, ensuring hemostasis during the adrenalectomy.
pathological confirmed hepatocellular carcinoma, IVC tumor thrombus and lymph node metastases.
